---
title: "Florida Woods Cockroach (Eurycotis floridana)"
slug: "eurycotis-floridana"
group: "Insecta"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622984389/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Florida Woods Cockroach"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Animal"
tax_class: "Insecta"
tax_order: "Blattodea"
tax_family: "Blattidae"
categories: ["Animal", "Insecta", "Blattodea", "Blattidae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622984389/medium.jpg" alt="Florida Woods Cockroach" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/jmull28">
jmull28</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342136309">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Insecta
-   **Order:** Blattodea
-   **Family:** Blattidae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=372251)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

Large, dark brown cockroach characterized by its robust body and ability to spray a foul-smelling defensive secretion. In Florida, it is commonly found in wooded habitats, living under logs, leaf litter, and bark rather than inside homes. It is primarily active at night and feeds on decaying plant material, playing an important role in nutrient recycling.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/372251-Eurycotis-floridana)
-   [Local profile](https://ask.ifas.ufl.edu/publication/IN915)
